name: \([[9,1,3]]\) Shor code
description:
Nine-qubit \hyperref[code:css]{CSS code} that is the first quantum error-correcting code \cite[ID 8802]{preset:qiskit}.
Among indecomposable \([[9,1,3]]\) CSS codes, the Shor code has the largest automorphism group \cite{arxiv:2501.17447}.

A set of logical codewords is
\begin{align}
\begin{split}
|\overline{0}\rangle&=\frac{1}{2\sqrt{2}}\left(|000\rangle+|111\rangle\right)^{\otimes3}\\
|\overline{1}\rangle&=\frac{1}{2\sqrt{2}}\left(|000\rangle-|111\rangle\right)^{\otimes3}~.
\end{split}
\end{align}
A stabilizer tableau for the code is
\begin{align}
\begin{array}{ccccccccc}
  Z & Z & I & I & I & I & I & I & I \\
  I & Z & Z & I & I & I & I & I & I \\
  I & I & I & Z & Z & I & I & I & I \\
  I & I & I & I & Z & Z & I & I & I \\
  I & I & I & I & I & I & Z & Z & I \\
  I & I & I & I & I & I & I & Z & Z \\
  X & X & X & X & X & X & I & I & I \\
  I & I & I & X & X & X & X & X & X
\end{array}~.
\end{align}
The \hyperref[topic:encoder-respecting]{encoder-respecting form} of the Shor code is a star-shaped tree graph \cite{arxiv:2411.14448}.
The code works by \hyperref[code:qubit_concatenated]{concatenating} each qubit of a phase-flip \hyperref[code:quantum_repetition]{repetition code} with a bit-flip \hyperref[code:quantum_repetition]{repetition code}. Therefore, the code can correct both types of errors simultaneously.
The code is degenerate: for example, two \(Z\) errors in the same three-qubit block act identically on all codewords \cite[Ch. 3]{preset:GottesmanBook}.

parents.detail: The Shor code is part of the sub-family of \([[m^2,1,m]]\) QPCs.
The Shor code is one of the nine-qubit surface codes defined on the projective plane \cite[Fig. 4]{arxiv:quant-ph/9810055}\cite[Fig. 20]{arxiv:quant-ph/0605094}.
The \([[9,1,3]]_{\mathbb{Z}_q}\) modular-qudit code for \(q=2\) reduces to the \([[9,1,3]]\) Shor code.

cousins.detail: The Shor code is a concatenation of a three-qubit bit-flip with a three-qubit phase-flip repetition code.
The Shor code is a concatenation of a three-qubit bit-flip with a three-qubit phase-flip repetition code.
The Shor code is the first quantum error-correcting code.
The Shor code admits a codeword that is the cluster state of a particular nine-vertex graph \cite{preset:GriffithsGS,arxiv:1511.05647}.

realizations: Trapped-ion qubits: state preparation with 98.8(1)\% and 98.5(1)\% fidelity for state \(|\overline{0}\rangle\) and \(|\overline{1}\rangle\), respectively, by N. Linke group \cite{arxiv:2104.01205}. Variants of the code to handle coherent noise studied and realized by K. Brown and C. Monroe groups \cite{arxiv:2105.05068}.

Optical systems: quantum teleportation of information implemented by J.-W. Pan group on a maximally entangled pair of one physical and one logical qubit with a fidelity of up to 78.6\% \cite{arxiv:2009.06242}. All-photonic quantum repeater architecture tested on the same code \cite{arxiv:2203.07979}.

name: \([[9,1,3]]\) Surface-17 code

cousins.detail: Both Shor's code and surface-17 are \([[9,1,3]]\) codes, but they are distinct (e.g., they have different \hyperref[topic:quantum-weight-enumerator]{quantum weight enumerators}).
Bring's code and the surface-17 code have been compared numerically \cite{arxiv:1712.07666}.

realizations:
Implemented at ETH Zurich by the Wallraff group
\cite{arxiv:2112.03708} and on the Zuchongzhi 2.1 superconducting quantum processor \cite{arxiv:2112.13505}.
Both experimental error rates are above the \hyperref[topic:pseudo-threshold]{pseudo-threshold} for this code relative to a single qubit; see Physics viewpoint for a summary \cite{doi:10.1103/Physics.15.103}.
Magic states have been created on the latter processor \cite{arxiv:2305.15972}.
Lattice surgery on the surface-17 code has been realized by splitting the code into two repetition codes by the Wallraff group \cite{arxiv:2501.04612}.
The device noise can be used to develop a decoder without relying on a theoretical noise model \cite{arxiv:2502.17722}.

name: \([[11,1,5]]\) quantum dodecacode
description:
Eleven-qubit \hyperref[topic:quantum-weight-enumerator]{pure} stabilizer code that is the smallest qubit stabilizer code to correct two-qubit errors.
It can be obtained from the non-additive dodecacode by puncturing \cite[Table IV]{arxiv:quant-ph/9608006}.

A stabilizer tableau for the code is given by \cite[Table 8.5]{arxiv:quant-ph/9705052}\cite{arxiv:quant-ph/0406063}
\begin{align}
\begin{array}{ccccccccccc}
  Z & Z & Z & Z & Z & Z & I & I & I & I & I \\
  X & X & X & X & X & X & I & I & I & I & I \\
  I & I & I & Z & X & Y & Y & Y & Y & X & Z \\
  I & I & I & X & Y & Z & Z & Z & Z & Y & X \\
  Z & Y & X & I & I & I & Z & Y & X & I & I \\
  X & Z & Y & I & I & I & X & Z & Y & I & I \\
  I & I & I & Z & Y & X & X & Y & Z & I & I \\
  I & I & I & X & Z & Y & Z & X & Y & I & I \\
  Z & X & Y & I & I & I & Z & Z & Z & X & Y \\
  Y & Z & X & I & I & I & Y & Y & Y & Z & X
\end{array}~.
\end{align}
The code has paired support despite not being Hermitian \cite{arxiv:2605.15344}.

name: \([[7,3,2]]\) punctured hypercube code

parents.detail: This code is the punctured version of the \([[8,3,2]]\) hypercube quantum code and is a phantom code \cite{arxiv:2601.20927}.
The punctured hypercube family is a quantum Reed-Muller family built from shortened and punctured classical Reed-Muller codes \cite{arxiv:2604.15111}.

cousins.detail: The \([[7,3,2]]\) code is obtained by puncturing one qubit from the \([[8,3,2]]\) hypercube quantum code \cite{arxiv:2601.20927}.
The \([[7,3,2]]\) punctured hypercube code \(H_X\) check matrix is the parity-check matrix of the \([7,4,3]\) Hamming code, while its \(H_Z\) matrix is that of the SPC code.
The \([[7,3,2]]\) punctured hypercube code \(H_X\) check matrix is the parity-check matrix of the \([7,4,3]\) Hamming code, while its \(H_Z\) matrix is that of the SPC code.

name: \(((7,2,3))\) Pollatsek-Ruskai code

parents.detail: The Pollatsek-Ruskai code is equivalent to the \(Q_{2,1,2,-}\) combinatorial PI code \cite[Sec. 5.2]{arxiv:2310.05358}. It is a seven-qubit PI code that realizes gates from the binary icosahedral group transversally.
The \(((7,2,3))\) Pollatsek-Ruskai code admits a transversal representation of the twisted \(1\)-group \(2I\) \cite{arxiv:2402.01638}.

cousins.detail: The Pollatsek-Ruskai code can be continuously deformed to the Steane code \cite{arxiv:2410.07983}.
Binary icosahedral group \(2I\) gates can be realized transversally in the Pollatsek-Ruskai code \cite{arxiv:2305.07023}.
The \(((7,2,3))\) Pollatsek-Ruskai code maps to the icosahedral spin code via the \hyperref[topic:dicke]{Dicke state mapping} \cite{arxiv:2305.07023}.
The \(((7,2,3))\) Pollatsek-Ruskai code maps to the icosahedral Fock-state code via the \hyperref[topic:qudit-dicke]{simplex mapping} \cite{arxiv:2509.20545}.

name: \([[7,2,2]]\) HGP phantom code

parents.detail: The \([[7,2,2]]\) HGP code is the smallest member of a simplex/repetition HGP family of CSS phantom codes \cite{arxiv:2601.20927}.
This code is the hypergraph product of the \([3,2,2]\) simplex code and the \([2,1,2]\) repetition code \cite{arxiv:2601.20927}.

cousins.detail: This code is constructed from the hypergraph product of the \([3,2,2]\) simplex code and the \([2,1,2]\) repetition code \cite{arxiv:2601.20927}.
This code is constructed from the hypergraph product of the \([3,2,2]\) simplex code and the \([2,1,2]\) repetition code \cite{arxiv:2601.20927}.

name: \([[7,1,3]]\) XZZX cyclic code
description:
A \([[7,1,3]]\) cyclic non-CSS code whose stabilizer generators are the seven cyclic shifts of the weight-four Pauli string \(XZIZXII\),
any six of which are independent.
The non-identity support of this generator is \(XZZX\) (at positions 0, 1, 3, 4), making this a member of the twisted XZZX code family.
It is one of sixteen distinct indecomposable \([[7,1,3]]\) codes \cite{arxiv:0709.1780}.

A stabilizer tableau for the code is given by \cite[ID 227]{preset:qiskit}
\begin{align}
\begin{array}{ccccccc}
  X & Z & I & Z & X & I & I \\
  I & X & Z & I & Z & X & I \\
  I & I & X & Z & I & Z & X \\
  X & I & I & X & Z & I & Z \\
  Z & X & I & I & X & Z & I \\
  I & Z & X & I & I & X & Z
\end{array}~.
\end{align}

parents.detail: The \([[7,1,3]]\) XZZX cyclic code is a cyclic non-CSS code whose generators are the cyclic shifts of the weight-four Pauli string \(XZIZXII\), which has \(XZZX\) support.

name: \([[7,1,3]]\) Steane code

parents.detail: Steane code is a 2D color code defined on a seven-qubit patch of the 6.6.6 tiling.
The Steane code is Hermitian \cite[ID 226]{preset:qiskit}\cite[Table 6]{arxiv:2501.17447}.
The Steane code is equivalent to a cyclic code via qubit permutations \cite[Exam. 1]{arxiv:1108.5490}.
The Steane code is a qubit quantum QR code \cite{arxiv:0712.0103,arxiv:1907.01393}.

There exists a set of stabilizer generators for the Steane code that make it a QDS code; a \([15,3]\) syndrome-measurement code beats five-fold repeated syndrome extraction at the same measurement cost \cite{arxiv:1409.2559,arxiv:1907.01393}.

The Steane code is the \(m=1\) member of the \([[2^{2m}+2^{m}+1,1,>2^{m}]]\) PG-QLDPC code family that is constructed from codes corresponding to lines and affine charts in \(PG(2,2^m)\) via the CSS construction \cite[Def. 4.9]{arxiv:1512.07081}.
The concatenated Steane code at level \(m=1\) is the Steane code.

The Steane code is the smallest heptagon holographic code. The encoding of more general heptagon holographic codes is a holographic tensor network consisting of the encoding isometry for the Steane code, which is a \hyperref[code:block_perfect]{planar-perfect tensor}.

cousins.detail: The Steane code is a group-representation code with \(G\) being the \(2O\) subgroup of \(SU(2)\) \cite{arxiv:2306.11621}.

The Steane code is equivalent via a single-qubit Clifford unitary to a cluster-state code for a particular graph and classical code \cite[Exam. 4]{arxiv:1108.5490}. Four non-isomorphic graphs yield graph quantum codes that are equivalent to the Steane code under a single-qubit-\hyperref[topic:clifford]{Clifford circuit} \cite{arxiv:quant-ph/0703112}.

The Steane code is globally equivalent to a \([[6,1,3;1]]\) EA CSS code, which the paper identifies as an example of the smallest one-ebit EA CSS code correcting an arbitrary single-qubit error on the sender's qubits \cite{arxiv:0803.1495}.

In Knill's \(C_4/C_6\) architecture, noisy \(\ket{\pi/8}\) states are injected using \(C_4/C_6\) logical Bell pairs and then purified by encoding them into the Steane code; Knill also proposed using the Steane code as a final concatenation level for the \(C_4/C_6\) scheme \cite{arxiv:quant-ph/0410199}.

A fault-tolerant logical \(T\) gate can be obtained by encoding the Steane code's seven physical qubits into the seven logical qubits of a \([[63,7,3]]\) outer quantum divisible CSS code preserved by transversal \(T^\dagger\) \cite{arxiv:2204.13176}.

realizations:
Trapped-ion devices: seven-qubit device in Blatt group \cite{arxiv:1403.5426}.
Ten-qubit QCCD device by Quantinuum \cite{arxiv:2107.07505} realizing repeated syndrome extraction, real-time look-up-table decoding (yielding lower logical SPAM error rate than physical SPAM), and non-fault-tolerant magic-state distillation (see APS Physics Synopsis \cite{doi:10.1103/Physics.14.184}).
Fault-tolerant universal two-qubit gate set using T injection by Monz group \cite{arxiv:2111.12654}.
Logical CNOT gate and Bell-pair creation between two logical qubits (yielding a logical fidelity higher than physical), including rounds of correction and fault-tolerant primitives such as flag qubits and pieceable fault tolerance, on a 20-qubit device by Quantinuum \cite{arxiv:2208.01863}; logical fidelity interval of the combined preparation-CNOT-measurement procedure was higher than that of the unencoded physical qubits.
Multiple rounds of Steane error correction \cite{arxiv:2312.09745}.
Fault-tolerant universal gate set via \hyperref[topic:code-switching]{code switching} between the Steane code and the \([[10,1,2]]\) code \cite{arxiv:2403.13732}.
Post-selected fault-tolerant logical Bell-state preparation with logical error rates at least 10 times lower than physical rate on a device by Quantinuum \cite{arxiv:2404.02280}.
The quantum Fourier transform on three code blocks \cite{arxiv:2404.08616}.
Fault-tolerant transversal and lattice-surgery state teleportation protocols as well as Knill error correction \cite{arxiv:2404.16728}.
Rains shadow enumerators have been measured \cite{arxiv:2408.16914}.
Inter-block CNOT gates have been characterized via cycle reconstruction \cite{arxiv:2504.12099}.
\hyperref[topic:code-switching]{Code switching} between the Steane code and the \([[15,1,3]]\) code as well as magic-state preparation and logical Bell measurements on the Steane code realized on the 28-qubit H2-1 device by Quantinuum \cite{arxiv:2506.14169}.
End-to-end fault-tolerant execution of QAOA and HHL circuits, including logical non-Clifford operations, with up to 12 logical qubits on Quantinuum systems \cite{arxiv:2603.04584}.

Neutral atom arrays: Lukin group. Ten logical qubits, transversal CNOT gate performed, logical ten-qubit GHZ state initialized with break-even fidelity, and fault-tolerant logical two-qubit GHZ state initialized \cite{arxiv:2312.03982}. Deep-circuit protocols with dozens of logical qubits and hundreds of logical teleportations \cite{arxiv:2506.20661}.

name: \([[16,6,4]]\) Tesseract color code

A (hyperbolic self-dual CSS) 4D color code defined on a tesseract, with stabilizer generators of both types supported on each cube. 
A \([[16,4,2,4]]\) tesseract subsystem code can be obtained from this code by using two logical qubits as gauge qubits \cite{arxiv:2409.04628}.

A stabilizer tableau for the code is \cite[ID 67d2cf9965e067195651cfbe]{preset:QECDB}

\begin{smallmatrix}
  X & X & X & X & X & X & X & X & X & X & X & X & X & X & X & X \\

  I & I & I & I & X & X & X & X & I & I & I & I & X & X & X & X \\
  I & I & I & I & I & I & I & I & X & X & X & X & X & X & X & X \\
  Z & Z & Z & Z & Z & Z & Z & Z & Z & Z & Z & Z & Z & Z & Z & Z \\
  I & Z & I & Z & I & Z & I & Z & I & Z & I & Z & I & Z & I & Z \\
  I & I & Z & Z & I & I & Z & Z & I & I & Z & Z & I & I & Z & Z \\
  I & I & I & I & Z & Z & Z & Z & I & I & I & I & Z & Z & Z & Z \\
  I & I & I & I & I & I & I & I & Z & Z & Z & Z & Z & Z & Z & Z
\end{smallmatrix}~.
\end{align}

parents.detail: The tesseract color code is a 4D color code defined on a tesseract \cite{arxiv:1703.00612,arXiv:1703.07847,arxiv:2008.05051,arxiv:2112.03785}.
The tesseract color code is constructed from the \([16,5,8]\) first-order RM code via the CSS construction \cite{arXiv:1703.07847,arxiv:2410.23263}.
The tesseract color code is a 4D color code defined on a tesseract \cite{arxiv:1703.00612,arXiv:1703.07847,arxiv:2008.05051,arxiv:2112.03785}.

cousins.detail: The tesseract color code is constructed from the \([16,5,8]\) first-order RM code via the CSS construction \cite{arXiv:1703.07847,arxiv:2410.23263}.
The \([[15,7,3]]\) quantum Hamming code can be obtained by puncturing the tesseract color code \cite{arxiv:2008.05051}.
Applying CNOT gates to the tesseract color code disentangles it into two \([[8,3,2]]\) color codes \cite{arxiv:2409.04628}.
The \([[16,4,2,4]]\) tesseract subsystem color code with particular gauge fixing can be obtained from four copies of the \([[4,2,2]]\) code \cite{arxiv:2409.04628}.
Stabilizer generators of both types of the tesseract color code are supported on each cube of a tesseract \cite{arxiv:2008.05051,arxiv:2112.03785}.

realizations: Trapped-ion devices: logical graph and GHZ states of up to 12 logical qubits constructed using three copies of the \([[16,4,2,4]]\) tesseract subsystem code, along with five rounds of post-selected fault-tolerant error correction in a device by Quantinuum \cite{arxiv:2409.04628}.
Neutral atom arrays: deep circuits and 1D-cluster-state creation using 96 logical qubits and hundreds of logical teleportations by the Lukin group \cite{arxiv:2506.20661}.

\end{smallmatrix}~.

name: \([[6,2,2]]\) \(C_6\) code

parents.detail: The \(C_6\) code is a color code on a ladder with three rungs and periodic boundary conditions, (a.k.a. a triangular prism with no top and bottom faces) \cite{preset:albertPC25}. Purely \(Z\)- or \(X\)-type stabilizers lie on the three square faces of the ladder.
The \([[k+4,k,2]]\) H code for \(k=2\) is the \(C_6\) code.
The Ganti-Onunkwo-Young code for \(r=1\) is the \(C_6\) code.
The Khesin-Lu-Shor code for \(r=2\) and \(m=2^r - 1 = 3\) is the \(C_6\) code.
The \(C_6\) code is Hermitian \cite[ID 126]{preset:qiskit}\cite[Table 6]{arxiv:2501.17447}.

cousins.detail: Fixing one logical qubit of the \([[6,2,2]]\) \(C_6\) code to \(|Y^{-}\rangle_L\) yields this \([[6,1,2]]\) code \cite[ID 59]{preset:qiskit}\cite{arxiv:2507.10519}.

Concatenations of \([[4,2,2]]\) and \(C_6\) codes yield fault-tolerant quantum computation schemes \cite{arxiv:quant-ph/0410199} admitting a post-selected threshold \cite{arxiv:quant-ph/0608018,arxiv:quant-ph/0703264} (see also Ref. \cite{arxiv:quant-ph/0612073}) and the Meier-Eastin-Knill (MEK) magic-state distillation protocols \cite{arxiv:1204.4221}. Concatenating quantum Hamming codes on top of the \([[4,2,2]]\) and \(C_6\) codes yields fault-tolerant quantum computation with constant space and quasi-polylogarithmic time overheads \cite{arxiv:2402.09606}. In the optimized protocol of Ref. \cite{arxiv:2402.09606}, a level-five \(C_4/C_6\) code underlies concatenated quantum Hamming codes \(\mathcal{Q}_5,\mathcal{Q}_6,\mathcal{Q}_7,\mathcal{Q}_7\), yielding a \(2.5\%\) threshold and space overheads \(162\) and \(373\) physical qubits per logical qubit at physical error rate \(0.1\%\) for logical CNOT error rates \(10^{-10}\) and \(10^{-24}\), respectively.

realizations: Trapped ions: fault-tolerant magic-state preparation demonstrated on a 20-qubit H1-1 device by Quantinuum \cite{arxiv:2506.14688}.

\end{array}~.

name: \(((6,2,3))\) transversal-\(\mathbb{Z}_{10}\) code
description:
Six-qubit code that realizes gates from the group \(\mathbb{Z}_{10}\) transversally.
This is the smallest known distance-three code supporting a transversal gate outside of the \hyperref[topic:clifford]{Clifford group}.
See Ref. \cite{arxiv:2504.20847} for the codewords.

name: \([[6,1,3]]\) Six-qubit stabilizer code
description:
A degenerate, non-trivial \([[6,1,3]]\) stabilizer code.
It is one of two six-qubit distance-three codes that are unique up to equivalence \cite{arxiv:quant-ph/9608006}, with the other code being decomposable and an extension of the five-qubit code \cite{arxiv:0803.1495}\cite[ID 87]{preset:qiskit}.
The code admits fault-tolerant syndrome extraction using only one ancilla per stabilizer generator measurement.

A stabilizer tableau for the code is given by \cite[ID 68]{preset:qiskit}
\begin{align}
\begin{array}{cccccc}
  X & I & I & I & I & Z \\
  I & X & X & Z & Z & I \\
  I & Y & I & Y & Z & Z \\
  I & X & Z & I & X & Z \\
  Z & Y & Z & Z & I & Y
\end{array}~.
\end{align}

Stabilizer generators and logical Pauli operators are presented in Refs. \cite{arxiv:0803.1495,arxiv:1008.0425}.
The code is equivalent to the graph code in Ref. \cite{arxiv:2501.12072}.

parents.detail: The \([[6,1,3]]\) six-qubit stabilizer code is the smallest six-qubit-tensor holographic code. The encoding of more general SCF holographic codes is a holographic tensor network consisting of the encoding isometry for the \([[6,1,3]]\) six-qubit stabilizer code.

cousins.detail: The \([[6,1,3]]\) six-qubit code can be converted into a \([[6,1,1,3]]\) subsystem code that saturates the subsystem Singleton bound while requiring only four stabilizer measurements during recovery \cite{arxiv:0803.1495,arxiv:1008.0425}.

The \([[6,1,3]]\) six-qubit code is one of two six-qubit distance-three codes that are unique up to equivalence \cite{arxiv:quant-ph/9608006}\cite[ID 68]{preset:qiskit}, with the other code being decomposable and an extension of the five-qubit code \cite{arxiv:0803.1495}\cite[ID 87]{preset:qiskit}.

name: \([[6,1,2]]\) semi-self-dual CSS code
description:
A six-qubit CSS stabilizer code that is an example of a semi-self-dual CSS code, i.e., a CSS code whose \(X\)-type stabilizers are contained in the \(Z\)-type stabilizers \cite{arxiv:2507.10519}.

A stabilizer tableau for the code is given by \cite[ID 59]{preset:qiskit}
\begin{align}
\begin{array}{cccccc}
  Z & I & Z & I & I & Z \\
  I & Z & Z & I & Z & I \\
  I & I & I & Z & Z & Z \\
  X & I & X & X & X & I \\
  I & X & X & X & I & X
\end{array}~.
\end{align}
An equivalent self-dual semi-CSS presentation has generators \(XXXXII\), \(IIXXXX\), \(ZZZZII\), \(IIZZZZ\), and \(IYIYIY\) \cite{arxiv:2507.10519}.
A qubit permutation \(0\mapsto 0\), \(1\mapsto 2\), \(2\mapsto 4\), \(3\mapsto 5\), \(4\mapsto 1\), \(5\mapsto 3\), followed by the same one-qubit Clifford on every qubit sending \(X\mapsto Z\), \(Z\mapsto Y\), and \(Y\mapsto X\), converts the CSS generators to that semi-CSS presentation.
This makes the code a concrete example of the equivalence between the semi-self-dual CSS and self-dual semi-CSS cases in the \(U(\ell,R_8)\) family \cite{arxiv:2507.10519}.

name: \([[6,4,2]]\) error-detecting code

parents.detail: The \([[2m,2m-2,2]]\) error-detecting code for \(m=3\) reduces to the \([[6,4,2]]\) error-detecting code.

The \([[6,4,2]]\) error-detecting code is a color code defined on a single hexagon of the 6.6.6 or 4.6.12 tilings. The \([[6,4,2]]\) code can be concatenated with the surface code to yield the 6.6.6 color code \cite[Appx. A]{arxiv:1604.04062}.
The \([[6,4,2]]\) error-detecting code is a color code defined on a single hexagon of the 6.6.6 or 4.6.12 tilings.

cousins.detail: Concatenations of this code with itself yield the level-\(r\) \([[6^r,4^r,2^r]]\) many-hypercube code \cite{arxiv:2403.16054}. The \([[6,4,2]]\) code can be concatenated with the surface code to yield the 6.6.6 color code \cite[Appx. A]{arxiv:1604.04062}.

realizations: Trapped-ion devices: Bayesian quantum phase estimation on a device by Quantinuum \cite{arxiv:2306.16608}.
Neutral atom arrays: state initialization of the \([[16,4,4]]\) doubly concatenated code (a.k.a., the level-two many-hypercube code) on a device by Infleqtion \cite{arxiv:2509.13247}.

name: \([[17,1,5]]\) 4.8.8 color code
description:
Seventeen-qubit doubly even 2D color code that admits a transversal implementation of the logical Clifford group.
The smallest distance-five CSS code has \(n=17\) \cite{arxiv:1207.6512}.
It is also a normal self-dual CSS code whose transversal Hadamard acts logically, making it suitable as an inner code for fifth-order magic-state distillation \cite{arxiv:1703.07847}.

A stabilizer tableau for the code is \cite[ID 672e4f2a4f33a38b84d76b7c]{preset:QECDB}

  Z & I & I & I & I & I & I & I & I & Z & I & I & Z & I & I & Z & I \\
  I & Z & I & I & I & I & I & I & I & Z & I & I & Z & I & I & I & Z \\

parents.detail: The \([[17,1,5]]\) color code can be defined on the 4.8.8 tiling \cite{arXiv:1509.03239}.
The smallest distance-five CSS code has \(n=17\) \cite{arxiv:1207.6512}.

name: \([[10,1,2]]\) Vasmer-Kubica code
description:
A stabilizer code obtained by morphing the \([[15,1,3]]\) quantum Reed-Muller code on a subset whose child code is the \([[8,3,2]]\) smallest interesting color code \cite{arxiv:2112.01446}.
It is the smallest known stabilizer code with a fault-tolerant logical \(T\) gate, implemented via physical \(T\), \(T^{\dagger}\), and \(CCZ\) gates \cite{arxiv:2112.01446}.

A stabilizer tableau for the code is \cite{arxiv:2403.13732}
\begin{align}
\begin{array}{cccccccccc}
  X & X & X & X & I & I & I & X & I & I \\
  I & X & X & I & X & X & I & I & X & I \\
  I & I & X & X & I & X & X & I & I & X \\
  Z & Z & Z & Z & I & I & I & I & I & I \\
  I & Z & Z & I & Z & Z & I & I & I & I \\
  I & I & Z & Z & I & Z & Z & I & I & I \\
  I & I & Z & I & I & Z & I & Z & I & I \\
  I & I & Z & Z & I & I & I & I & Z & I \\
  I & Z & Z & I & I & I & I & I & I & Z
\end{array}~.
\end{align}
The minimum-weight logical-\(Z\) representatives are \(Z_1 Z_8\), \(Z_5 Z_9\), and \(Z_7 Z_{10}\), each of weight two, confirming \(d=2\).

parents.detail: The \([[10,1,2]]\) code is a specific instance of the \([[2^r+r-1,1,2]]\) morphed simplex codes with \(r=3\) \cite[Appx. C]{arxiv:2112.01446}.

cousins.detail: The \([[10,1,2]]\) code is obtained by morphing the \([[15,1,3]]\) code on a region whose child code is the \([[8,3,2]]\) smallest interesting color code \cite{arxiv:2112.01446}.
The \([[10,1,2]]\) code is obtained by morphing the \([[15,1,3]]\) code on a region whose child code is the \([[8,3,2]]\) smallest interesting color code \cite{arxiv:2112.01446}.
A fault-tolerant universal gate set can be obtained via \hyperref[topic:code-switching]{code switching} between the Steane code and the \([[10,1,2]]\) code \cite{arxiv:2403.13732}.

realizations: Trapped-ion devices: fault-tolerant universal gate set via \hyperref[topic:code-switching]{code switching} between the Steane code and the \([[10,1,2]]\) code on a device from the Monz group \cite{arxiv:2403.13732}.

name: \([[10,2,3]]\) rotated toric code
description:
Rotated toric code that is the CSS form of the twisted XZZX toric code with parameters \(a=1\), \(b=3\) \cite{arxiv:1108.5490,arxiv:2101.09349},
related to the XZZX form by Hadamard on the \(B\)-sublattice.
It is also the \hyperref[topic:symplectic-doubling]{symplectic double} (a.k.a. genus-one double cover) of the \([[5,1,3]]\) five-qubit perfect code \cite{arxiv:1212.6703,arxiv:2406.09951}, the \hyperref[topic:symplectic-doubling]{symplectic double} of the \([[5,1,2]]\) rotated surface code \cite{arxiv:2406.09951}, and a BCC code \cite{arxiv:2509.06865}.

A stabilizer tableau for this code is
\begin{align}
\begin{array}{cccccccccc}
  Z & Z & I & Z & Z & I & I & I & I & I \\
  I & I & Z & Z & I & Z & Z & I & I & I \\
  I & I & I & I & Z & Z & I & Z & Z & I \\
  Z & I & I & I & I & I & Z & Z & I & Z \\
  X & I & X & X & I & I & I & I & I & X \\
  I & X & X & I & X & X & I & I & I & I \\
  I & I & I & X & X & I & X & X & I & I \\
  I & I & I & I & I & X & X & I & X & X
\end{array}~.
\end{align}

parents.detail: This is the \(d=3\) instance of the \([[d^2+1,2,d]]\) family of twisted XZZX toric codes (parameters \(a=1\), \(b=3\)), presented in its CSS form \cite{arxiv:2509.06865}.

The \([[10,2,3]]\) rotated toric code is a \([[d^2+1,2,d]]\) BCC code for \(d=3\) \cite{arxiv:2509.06865}. A non-CSS cyclic cluster code related to the \([[10,2,3]]\) rotated toric code yields the \([[5,1,3]]\) five-qubit perfect code for \(d=3\) \cite{arxiv:2509.06865}.

cousins.detail: The \([[10,2,3]]\) rotated toric code is the \hyperref[topic:symplectic-doubling]{symplectic double} (a.k.a. genus-one double cover) of the five-qubit perfect code \cite[Exam. 3]{arxiv:1212.6703}\cite{arxiv:2406.09951}. A non-CSS cyclic cluster code related to the \([[10,2,3]]\) rotated toric code yields the \([[5,1,3]]\) five-qubit perfect code for \(d=3\) \cite{arxiv:2509.06865}.
The \([[10,2,3]]\) rotated toric code is the \hyperref[topic:symplectic-doubling]{symplectic double} (a.k.a. genus-one double cover) of the \([[5,1,2]]\) rotated surface code \cite{arxiv:2406.09951}.

name: \([[10,2,3]]\) binarized Galois-qudit code
description:
CSS code obtained by binarizing a \([[5,1,3]]_4\) Galois-qudit CSS code in the self-dual normal basis \(\{\omega,\omega^2\}\).

A stabilizer tableau for the binarized code is \cite{arxiv:2601.20927}
\begin{align}
\begin{array}{cccccccccc}
  Z & I & Z & I & Z & I & Z & I & I & I \\
  I & Z & I & Z & I & Z & I & Z & I & I \\
  I & I & Z & I & Z & Z & I & Z & Z & I \\
  I & I & I & Z & Z & I & Z & Z & I & Z \\
  X & I & X & I & X & I & X & I & I & I \\
  I & X & I & X & I & X & I & X & I & I \\
  I & I & X & I & I & X & X & X & X & I \\
  I & I & I & X & X & X & X & I & I & X
\end{array}~.
\end{align}

cousins.detail: The binarized \([[10,2,3]]\) code is not phantom, but concatenating each qubit pair with the \([[4,2,2]]\) code yields a \([[20,2,6]]\) B\&C phantom code admitting fold-diagonal logical \(SS\) gates \cite{arxiv:2601.20927}.
Concatenating each qubit pair of the binarized \([[10,2,3]]\) code with the \([[4,2,2]]\) code yields the \([[20,2,6]]\) B\&C phantom code \cite{arxiv:2601.20927,arxiv:2605.15344}.

name: \(((10,24,3))\) qubit code
description:
Ten-qubit nonadditive CWS code saturating the linear-programming bound for one-error-correcting codes on ten qubits \cite{arxiv:0709.1780}.
It was constructed using the coding-clique graph-theoretic framework introduced in \cite{arxiv:0709.1780}, which unifies additive and nonadditive code constructions.

The \(((10,24,3))\) qubit code can be combined to form an infinite family of distance-three qubit codes whose logical dimension is \(50\%\) larger than that of the optimal stabilizer code \cite{arxiv:0901.1935}.

parents.detail: The \(((10,24,3))\) qubit code is a CWS code \cite{preset:Cross09}.

The \(((10,24,3))\) qubit code can be combined to form an infinite family of distance-three qubit codes whose logical dimension is \(50\%\) larger than that of the optimal stabilizer code \cite{arxiv:0901.1935}.

name: \(((8,2,3))\) Plenio-Vedral-Knight CE code
\([[8,2,3]]\) Hermitian code
description:
A non-CSS Hermitian eight-qubit stabilizer code that is the only Hermitian code and that has the largest automorphism group among the 20 inequivalent \([[8,2,3]]\) codes \cite{arxiv:2501.17447}.
The code has an exceptionally short fault-tolerant syndrome measurement sequence \cite{arxiv:2008.05051,arxiv:2501.17447}.

A stabilizer tableau for the code is \cite{arxiv:2605.15344}
\begin{align}
\begin{array}{cccccccc}
  X & X & X & X & I & I & I & I \\
  Z & Z & Z & Z & I & I & I & I \\
  I & I & I & I & X & X & X & X \\
  I & I & I & I & Z & Z & Z & Z \\
  I & X & Y & Z & I & X & Y & Z \\
  I & Z & X & Y & I & Z & X & Y
\end{array}~,
\end{align}
which is equivalent to \cite[ID 4947]{preset:qiskit}.

cousins.detail: Applying the BLT mapping to the \([[8,2,3]]\) Hermitian code and concatenating each qubit pair with the \([[4,2,2]]\) code yields a \([[32,4,6]]\) self-dual CSS code \cite[Corr. 2]{arxiv:2605.15344}.
Applying the BLT mapping to the \([[8,2,3]]\) Hermitian code and concatenating each qubit pair with the \([[4,2,2]]\) code yields a \([[32,4,6]]\) self-dual CSS code \cite[Corr. 2]{arxiv:2605.15344}.
Applying the BLT mapping to the \([[8,2,3]]\) Hermitian code and concatenating each qubit pair with the \([[4,2,2]]\) code yields a \([[32,4,6]]\) self-dual CSS code \cite[Corr. 2]{arxiv:2605.15344}.

parents.detail: The surface code on a cube is a twist-defect surface code whose degree-three vertices can be interpreted as disclination twists \cite{arxiv:2010.06628}.

cousins.detail: The surface code on a cube, whose qubits lie on the vertices of a cube, is obtained by three-coloring the faces of a cube and placing \(X\), \(Y\), and \(Z\) stabilizer generators on each pair of faces of the same color.

  X & X & I & I & I & I & X & X
\end{array}~.

name: \([[8,1,2]]\) Shen-Wang-Cao code
description:
A stabilizer code that admits a logical \(T\) gate via application of physical \(T\) gates and a \(CZ\)-like gate.

A stabilizer tableau for the code is given by \cite[ID 980]{preset:qiskit}.
\begin{align}
\begin{array}{cccccccc}
  X & X & I & I & X & X & I & I \\
  I & I & X & X & X & X & I & I \\
  I & I & I & I & I & I & X & X \\
  Z & I & I & Z & I & Z & Z & Z \\
  I & Z & I & Z & I & Z & Z & Z \\
  I & I & Z & Z & I & I & I & I \\
  I & I & I & I & Z & Z & I & I
\end{array}~.
\end{align}

cousins.detail: The \([[8,1,2]]\) code is an XP-regular code that can be obtained via the XP stabilizer formalism applied to the \([[15,1,3]]\) Reed-Muller code \cite{arxiv:2310.19538}.
The \([[8,1,2]]\) code is an XP-regular code that can be obtained via the XP stabilizer formalism applied to the \([[15,1,3]]\) Reed-Muller code \cite{arxiv:2310.19538}.
The \([[8,1,2]]\) code is an XP-regular code that can be obtained via the XP stabilizer formalism applied to the \([[15,1,3]]\) Reed-Muller code \cite{arxiv:2310.19538}.

name: \([[8, 3, 3]]\) Eight-qubit Gottesman code
description:
Eight-qubit \hyperref[topic:degeneracy]{non-degenerate} code that can be obtained from a modified CSS construction using the \([8,4,4]\) extended Hamming code and a \([8,7,2]\) even-weight code \cite{arxiv:quant-ph/9605021}.
The modification introduces signs between the codewords.

See \cite[Table 3.3]{arxiv:quant-ph/9705052} for its stabilizer generator matrix.
A stabilizer tableau for the code is given by \cite[ID 6822]{preset:qiskit}
\begin{align}
\begin{array}{cccccccc}
  X & Y & Y & X & Z & I & I & Z \\
  Z & X & I & Z & X & I & Z & Z \\
  X & Z & Z & I & I & X & Z & Z \\
  Y & I & Y & Z & I & Z & X & Z \\
  Y & Z & X & I & Z & Z & I & Y
\end{array}~.
\end{align}
The code's automorphism group is \(\text{A}\Gamma\text{L}(1,8)\) \cite{arxiv:2109.12735}.
It is unique for its parameters, up to equivalence \cite{arxiv:0709.1780}\cite[pg. 386]{doi:10.1007/3-540-30731-1}.

cousins.detail: The \([[8, 3, 3]]\) code is obtained via a modified CSS construction from the \([8,4,4]\) extended Hamming code.

align_blocks:
\begin{array}{cccccccc}
  X & Y & Y & X & Z & I & I & Z \\
  Z & X & I & Z & X & I & Z & Z \\
  X & Z & Z & I & I & X & Z & Z \\
  Y & I & Y & Z & I & Z & X & Z \\
  Y & Z & X & I & Z & Z & I & Y
\end{array}~.

name: \([[8,3,2]]\) Smallest interesting color code

parents.detail: The \([[8,3,2]]\) code is the smallest non-trivial 3D color code.
The \([[8,3,2]]\) code is a hypercube code for \(D=3\).
The \([[8,3,2]]\) code is the \(k=1\) member of the \([[6k+2,3k,2]]\) Campbell-Howard family with a quasi-transversal logical \(CCZ\) gate \cite{arxiv:1606.01904}.

cousins.detail: The \([[8,3,2]]\) hypercube code \(H_X\) check matrix is the parity-check matrix of the \([8,4,4]\) extended Hamming code, while its \(H_Z\) matrix is that of the SPC code.
The \([[8,3,2]]\) hypercube code \(H_X\) check matrix is the parity-check matrix of the \([8,4,4]\) extended Hamming code, while its \(H_Z\) matrix is that of the SPC code.
As the \(D=3\) member of the hypercube-code family, the \([[8,3,2]]\) code can be viewed as an XP stabilizer code with precision \(N=8\) \cite[Exam. 6.10]{arxiv:2203.00103}.
The \([[8,3,2]]\) code can be obtained from a subset of physical qubits of the \([[15,1,3]]\) code \cite{arxiv:2112.01446}.

Three cyclically rotated copies of the 3D surface/toric code admit a logical \(CCZ\) gate via transversal physical \(CCZ\) gates, and concatenating each such qubit triple with an \([[8,3,2]]\) block yields a 3D toric/color family with parameters \([[8n,3,2d]]\); its smallest member has parameters \([[72,3,4]]\) \cite{arxiv:2404.19005}.

Concatenating \([[8,3,2]]\) blocks with triples of qubits drawn from three cyclically rotated 3D surface/toric codes yields a 3D toric/color family with parameters \([[8n,3,2d]]\) and transversal logical \(CCZ\) implemented by physical \(T\) gates on the inner \([[8,3,2]]\) blocks \cite{arxiv:2404.19005}.

realizations: Trapped ions: one-qubit addition algorithm implemented fault-tolerantly on the Quantinuum H1-1 device \cite{arxiv:2309.09893}. Trapped-ion processor by AQT: measurement-free universal fault-tolerant logical operations and a Grover-search demonstration \cite{arxiv:2506.22600}.
Superconducting circuits: fault-tolerant \(CCZ\) gate performed on IBM and IonQ devices \cite{arxiv:2309.08663}.

Neutral atom arrays: Lukin group \cite{arxiv:2312.03982}. 48 logical qubits, 228 logical two-qubit gates, 48 logical \(CCZ\) gates, and error detection performed in 16 blocks. Circuit outcomes were sampled and cross-entropy (XEB) was calculated to verify quantumness. Logical entanglement entropy was measured \cite{arxiv:2312.03982}.

\begin{array}{cccccccc}

  Z & I & Z & Z & I & I & Z & I \\
  I & I & Z & I & Z & I & Z & Z \\
  Z & I & Z & I & I & Z & I & Z \\
  X & X & X & X & X & X & X & X
\end{array}~.

name: \(((8,16,2))\) \(PG(3,2)\) code
description: Eight-qubit code encoding four logical qubits whose logical basis consists of a GHZ state together with fifteen states built from the incidence geometry of the projective space \(PG(3,2)\).

cousins.detail: This is the exceptional nonstabilizer \(k=4\) qubit phantom code of minimal length eight that violates the generic bound \(n\geq 2^k-1\) \cite{arxiv:2604.15111}.
The logical basis of the \(((8,16,2))\) \(PG(3,2)\) code contains a GHZ state and linear combinations of self-complementary states \cite{arxiv:2604.15111}.

name: \(((4,2,2))\) Four-qubit single-deletion code

parents.detail: The four-qubit single-deletion code is a GNU code for \(g=m=2\) \cite{arxiv:2102.02494}.
The Bravyi-Lee-Li-Yoshida code reduces to the four-qubit single-deletion code for \(n=4\).

cousins.detail: The four-qubit single-deletion code can be obtained from the "0-2-4" single-mode binomial code by substituting Fock states with \hyperref[topic:dicke]{Dicke states}.

Projecting the four-qubit code into the PI subspace yields the four-qubit single-deletion code. A basis of codewords for the four-qubit single-deletion code consists of the \(|\overline{00}\rangle\) and \(|\overline{01}\rangle+|\overline{10}\rangle+|\overline{11}\rangle\) states of the four-qubit code.
The combinatorial PI code \(Q_{1,1,1,-}\) is another example of a four-qubit code correcting a single deletion error \cite[Sec. 5.1]{arxiv:2310.05358}.

name: Numerically optimized four-qubit AD code

parents.detail: Numerically optimized four-qubit AD codes can be obtained from a biconvex optimization of the entanglement fidelity \cite{arxiv:2411.12952}.

cousins.detail: The numerically optimized four-qubit AD code can correct a single \hyperref[topic:ad]{AD} error with higher entanglement fidelity than the \([[4,1,2]]\) LNCY code \cite{arxiv:quant-ph/9704002}.
Numerically optimized four-qubit AD codes can be obtained from a machine-learning optimization \cite{arxiv:2503.11783}.

name: \([[4,2,2]]\) Four-qubit code

parents.detail: The \([[4,2,2]]\) code is the smallest rotated toric code \cite{arxiv:2501.17447}.

The \([[4,2,2]]\) code can be interpreted as a 2D color code on a square of the 4.8.8 tiling \cite{arxiv:2212.00042,arxiv:2305.13581}. Removing \(X\) checks from blue octagons and \(Z\) checks from green octagons of the 4.8.8 color code yields a light 4.8.8 color code that is equivalent to concatenating the surface/toric code with the \([[4,2,2]]\) code \cite{arxiv:1604.04062}.
The \([[4,2,2]]\) code can be interpreted as a 2D color code on a trapezoidal patch that makes up two-thirds of a hexagon of the 6.6.6 tiling \cite{arxiv:2212.00042,arxiv:2305.13581}.

The \([[4,2,2]]\) code can be interpreted as a 2D color code on a square of the 4.6.12 tiling \cite{arxiv:2212.00042,arxiv:2305.13581}. Concatenating the \([[4,2,2]]\) code with two copies of the surface code on a hexagonal lattice yields the self-dual 4.6.12 color code \cite{arxiv:1604.04062}.
The \([[4,2,2]]\) code is a hypercube code for \(D=2\).
The \([[2m,2m-2,2]]\) error-detecting code for \(m=2\) reduces to the \([[4,2,2]]\) code.
The \([[4,2,2]]\) code can be interpreted as a brickwork code on a square of the overlapping rectangular tilings \cite{arxiv:2503.15751}.
The four group-qudit code reduces to the four-qubit code for \(G=\mathbb{Z}_2\).

cousins.detail: The \([[4,2,2]]\) code can be derived from the five-qubit code using a protocol that converts an \([[n,k,d]]\) code into an \([[n-1, k+1, d-1]]\) code \cite[Sec. 3.5]{arxiv:quant-ph/9705052}\cite[Fig. 3]{arxiv:1907.11253}.
Concatenating the \([[4,2,2]]\) code with the surface code is equivalent to removing stabilizer generators from the 4.8.8 color code \cite{arxiv:1604.04062}.
The toric code can be constructed by arranging \([[4,2,2]]\) tensors on a square lattice and recovering the star and plaquette operators by operator pushing \cite{arxiv:2109.08158}.

Concatenations of \([[4,2,2]]\) and \(C_6\) codes yield fault-tolerant quantum computation schemes \cite{arxiv:quant-ph/0410199} admitting a post-selected threshold \cite{arxiv:quant-ph/0608018,arxiv:quant-ph/0703264} (see also Ref. \cite{arxiv:quant-ph/0612073}).
Concatenating quantum Hamming codes on top of the \([[4,2,2]]\) and \(C_6\) codes yields fault-tolerant quantum computation with constant space and quasi-polylogarithmic time overheads \cite{arxiv:2402.09606}. In the optimized protocol of Ref. \cite{arxiv:2402.09606}, a level-five \(C_4/C_6\) code underlies concatenated quantum Hamming codes \(\mathcal{Q}_5,\mathcal{Q}_6,\mathcal{Q}_7,\mathcal{Q}_7\), yielding a \(2.5\%\) threshold and space overheads \(162\) and \(373\) physical qubits per logical qubit at physical error rate \(0.1\%\) for logical CNOT error rates \(10^{-10}\) and \(10^{-24}\), respectively.
Concatenating the \([[4,2,2]]\) code with the surface code is equivalent to removing stabilizer generators from the 4.8.8 color code \cite{arxiv:1604.04062}.
The \([[4,2,2]]\) code can be concatenated with two copies of the surface code to yield the 4.6.12 color code \cite{arxiv:1604.04062}.

Concatenations of \([[4,2,2]]\) and \(C_6\) codes yield fault-tolerant quantum computation schemes \cite{arxiv:quant-ph/0410199} admitting a post-selected threshold \cite{arxiv:quant-ph/0608018,arxiv:quant-ph/0703264} (see also Ref. \cite{arxiv:quant-ph/0612073}) and the Meier-Eastin-Knill (MEK) magic-state distillation protocols \cite{arxiv:1204.4221}. Concatenating quantum Hamming codes on top of the \([[4,2,2]]\) and \(C_6\) codes yields fault-tolerant quantum computation with constant space and quasi-polylogarithmic time overheads \cite{arxiv:2402.09606}. In the optimized protocol of Ref. \cite{arxiv:2402.09606}, a level-five \(C_4/C_6\) code underlies concatenated quantum Hamming codes \(\mathcal{Q}_5,\mathcal{Q}_6,\mathcal{Q}_7,\mathcal{Q}_7\), yielding a \(2.5\%\) threshold and space overheads \(162\) and \(373\) physical qubits per logical qubit at physical error rate \(0.1\%\) for logical CNOT error rates \(10^{-10}\) and \(10^{-24}\), respectively.

The Steane code can be built from two \([[4,2,2]]\) codes in the quantum Lego code framework \cite{arxiv:2109.08158}. Ref. \cite{arxiv:2402.09606} also introduces a \(C_4\)/Steane concatenated code, obtained by concatenating the \([[4,2,2]]\) code with the Steane code, as an underlying code for further concatenation with quantum Hamming codes.
The \([[6,4,2]]\) error-detecting code can be constructed out of two \([[4,2,2]]\) codes in the quantum Lego code framework \cite{arxiv:2109.08158}.
CPC gadgets for the \([[4,2,2]]\) code have been implemented on the IBM 5Q superconducting device \cite{arxiv:1709.01866}.
A \(((4,3,1))_2\) jump code is a subcode of the \([[4,2,2]]\) code and contains the \([[4,1,2]]\) LNCY code as a subcode \cite{arxiv:quant-ph/0208140}.

The \([[4,2,2]]\) code is the smallest phantom code: logical CNOT gates between its two logical qubits can be implemented by physical-qubit permutations \cite{arxiv:2601.20927}. Gluing copies of the \([[4,2,2]]\) code with \(X\)-type stabilizers yields CSS phantom codes with parameters \([[4m,2,(d_X=2,d_Z=2m)]]\), and puncturing one qubit from this construction yields \([[4m-1,2,(d_X=2,d_Z=2m-1)]]\), for \(m\geq1\) \cite{arxiv:2601.20927}.

realizations: See also \cite[Tab. I]{arxiv:2412.07670} for more details on each experimental realization.
Trapped-ion device by IonQ \cite{arxiv:1611.06946}.
Logical state preparation and flag-qubit error correction realized in superconducting-circuit devices by IBM \cite{arxiv:1705.09259,arxiv:1705.08957,arxiv:1806.02359,arxiv:2110.04285}.
The CZ magic state has been realized on an IBM heavy-hex superconducting circuit device \cite{arxiv:2305.13581}.
CPC gadgets for the \([[4,2,2]]\) code have been implemented on the IBM 5Q superconducting device \cite{arxiv:1709.01866}.
An FPGA implementation of the collision clustering decoder \cite{arxiv:2309.05558} realized on a Rigetti superconducting device \cite{arxiv:2410.05202}.

Neutral atom arrays: error detection, erasure correction, and post-selected fault-tolerant circuits demonstrated on 24 logical qubits on a 256-qubit device by Atom Computing, with each qubit encoded in the \([[4,2,2]]\) code \cite{arxiv:2411.11822}. 
Post-selected fault-tolerant realization of a benchmarking protocol \cite{arxiv:1610.03507}, preparation of the ground state of the single-impurity Anderson impurity model, and post-selected fault-tolerant logical Bell-state preparation demonstrated on one copy of the \([[4,2,2]]\) code on a device by Infleqtion \cite{arxiv:2412.07670}.
Error correction with mid-circuit erasure measurements and logical teleportation demonstrated by the Thompson group \cite{arxiv:2506.13724}. Logical implementation of Shor's algorithm on a device by Infleqtion \cite{arxiv:2509.13247}.
The \([[4,2,2]]\) code has been implemented on a star topology using superconducting devices and microwave cavities \cite{arxiv:2503.12869}.
Trapped-ion processor by AQT: modular logical-state teleportation between two four-qubit error-detecting code blocks without mid-circuit measurements \cite{arxiv:2506.22600}.

\begin{array}{cccc}
  Z & Z & Z & Z \\
  X & X & X & X
\end{array}~.

name: \([[4,1,2]]\) Leung-Nielsen-Chuang-Yamamoto (LNCY) code

parents.detail: The \([[4,1,2]]\) LNCY code is a small planar rotated surface code \cite{arxiv:2006.03071,arxiv:1912.09410,arxiv:2102.06132,arxiv:2102.13071}.

The \([[4,1,2]]\) LNCY code is the smallest QPC, i.e., a concatenation of a two-qubit bit-flip with a two-qubit phase-flip repetition code.
An \([[8,1,2]]\) QPC correcting a single \hyperref[topic:ad]{AD} error is equivalent to a concatenation of its constant-excitation version with the dual-rail code \cite{arxiv:quant-ph/0103042,arxiv:quant-ph/0501184,arxiv:2010.00538}.

The \([[4,1,2]]\) LNCY code (approximately) corrects a single \hyperref[topic:ad]{AD} error \cite{arxiv:quant-ph/9704002} and is the smallest member of the amplitude-damping stabilizer family of Ref. \cite{arxiv:0710.1052}.

The \([[4,1,2]]\) LNCY code \cite{arxiv:quant-ph/0103042} is equivalent to a \(((4,2,1))_2\) jump code correcting a single \hyperref[topic:ad]{AD} error.
A \(((4,3,1))_2\) jump code is a subcode of the \([[4,2,2]]\) code and contains the \([[4,1,2]]\) LNCY code as a subcode \cite{arxiv:quant-ph/0208140}.

cousins.detail: The \([[4,1,2]]\) LNCY code is obtained as the \(\{|\overline{00}\rangle,|\overline{01}\rangle\}\) \([[4,1,2]]\) subcode of the \([[4,2,2]]\) four-qubit code \cite{arxiv:quant-ph/9704002}. A \(((4,3,1))_2\) jump code is a subcode of the \([[4,2,2]]\) code and contains the \([[4,1,2]]\) LNCY code as a subcode \cite{arxiv:quant-ph/0208140}.
Adding \(XXII\) (\(XYZI\)) to the stabilizer group of the \([[4,2,2]]\) code yields the \([[4,1,2]]\) LNCY (twist-defect) code.

The \([[4,1,2]]\) LNCY code reduces to the \(0,2,4\) binomial code when the basis labels in each codeword are written as in base-ten. Such a mapping can be generalized \cite{manual:{L. Li, private communication, 2018}}.

Magic states prepared using a \([[4,1,2]]\) subcode can be injected into the heavy-hex code \cite{arxiv:2110.04285,arxiv:2305.13581}.
The \(d=2\) heavy-hex code is closely related to the \([[4,1,2]]\) LNCY code.

The \([[4,1,2]]\) LNCY code is the smallest QPC, i.e., a concatenation of a two-qubit bit-flip with a two-qubit phase-flip repetition code.
An \([[8,1,2]]\) QPC correcting a single \hyperref[topic:ad]{AD} error is equivalent to a concatenation of its constant-excitation version with the dual-rail code \cite{arxiv:quant-ph/0103042,arxiv:quant-ph/0501184,arxiv:2010.00538}.
More generally, an \([[m^2,1,m]]\) QPC corrects \(m-1\) \hyperref[topic:ad]{AD} errors \cite{arxiv:1001.2356}.
Recursively concatenating a \([[4,1,2]]\) LNCY subcode attains a threshold \cite{arxiv:0902.2658,arxiv:0902.4506}.
A \([[4,1,2]]\) LNCY code can be thought of as a cluster-state code \cite{arxiv:1407.2777}.

The \([[4,1,2]]\) LNCY code is a small quantum polar encoding \cite{manual:{K. Noh, “Leung code as quantum polar code”, 2017 \href{https://github.com/errorcorrectionzoo/eczoo_data/files/7652763/Leung_code_as_quantum_polar_code.pdf}{URL}}}.

realizations: Linear optical networks \cite{arxiv:0804.2268,arxiv:1404.5498}.
Superconducting-circuit devices \cite{arxiv:1912.09410,arxiv:2102.06132}.

Logical gates within one block \cite{arxiv:2102.13071} and between two blocks \cite{arxiv:2006.03071}, with the latter interpreted as lattice surgery between planar surface codes, were realized in superconducting circuits.
Neutral atom arrays by Atom Computing ran the Bernstein-Vazirani algorithm on up to 28 logical qubits \cite{arxiv:2411.11822}.
Break-even performance has been demonstrated on a superconducting IBM device using the syndrome-based Petz recovery \cite{arxiv:2510.08719}.
Implementation of a universal logical gate set in superconducting circuits by Origin Quantum Computing \cite{arxiv:2405.09035}.

\begin{array}{cccc}

  Z & Z & Z & Z
\end{array}~.
|\overline{\pm}\rangle = \frac{1}{2}(|00\rangle \pm |11\rangle)^{\otimes 2}~.

name: \([[4,1,2]]\) twist-defect code

parents.detail: The \([[4,1,2]]\) twist-defect code is the smallest triangular color code with \(x\)-, \(y\)-, and \(z\)-type Pauli boundaries, which make the code non-CSS \cite[Fig. 7]{arxiv:1806.02820}.

The \([[4,1,2]]\) twist-defect code is equivalent to a twist-defect surface code on a tetrahedron inscribed in a sphere \cite{arxiv:2406.09951} via a single-qubit \hyperref[topic:clifford]{Clifford circuit}.

cousins.detail: Adding \(XYZI\) to the stabilizer group of the \([[4,2,2]]\) four-qubit code yields the \([[4,1,2]]\) twist-defect subcode \cite{arxiv:quant-ph/0512170}.
The \hyperref[topic:symplectic-doubling]{symplectic double} of the \([[4,1,2]]\) twist-defect code is the \([[8,2,2]]\) twisted toric code \cite{arxiv:2406.09951}.

realizations: Logical \hyperref[topic:clifford]{Clifford gates} were realized in a trapped-ion device by Quantinuum \cite{arxiv:2406.09951}.

align_blocks:
\begin{array}{cccc}
  X & I & X & X \\
  Y & Y & I & Y \\
  Z & Z & Z & I
\end{array}~.

name: \([[15,1,3]]\) quantum RM code
description:
A \([[15,1,3]]\) quantum Reed-Muller code that is most easily thought of as a tetrahedral 3D color code.
It can be constructed as a CSS code from the \([15,5,8]\) punctured Reed-Muller code and its even subcode, which explains its transversal \(T^\dagger\) gate \cite{preset:GottesmanBook}.

This code contains 15 qubits, represented by four vertices, four face centers, six edge centers, and one body center.
The tetrahedron is cellulated into four identical polyhedron cells by connecting the body center to all four face centers, where each face center is then connected by three adjacent edge centers.
Each colored cell corresponds to a weight-eight \(X\)-check, and each face corresponds to a weight-4 \(Z\)-check.
A logical \(Z\) is any weight-3 \(Z\)-string along an edge of the entire tetrahedron.
The logical \(X\) is any weight-7 \(X\)-face of the entire tetrahedron.

The code can also be thought of as a code on all corners of a tesseract except one \cite{doi:10.1098/rsta.2011.0494}.

In the qubit order given by the nonzero binary four-tuples \(0001,0010,\ldots,1111\), one stabilizer tableau for the code is \cite[ID 6705228b19cca60cf657a8f6]{preset:QECDB}
\begin{align}
\begin{smallmatrix}
  Z & Z & I & Z & I & I & Z & I & I & I & I & I & I & I & I \\
  Z & Z & I & I & Z & Z & I & I & I & I & I & I & I & I & I \\
  Z & Z & I & I & I & I & I & Z & I & I & Z & I & I & I & I \\
  Z & Z & I & I & I & I & I & I & Z & Z & I & I & I & I & I \\
  Z & Z & I & I & I & I & I & I & I & I & I & Z & I & I & Z \\
  Z & Z & I & I & I & I & I & I & I & I & I & I & Z & Z & I \\
  Z & I & Z & Z & I & Z & I & I & I & I & I & I & I & I & I \\
  Z & I & Z & I & I & I & I & Z & I & Z & I & I & I & I & I \\
  Z & I & Z & I & I & I & I & I & I & I & I & Z & I & Z & I \\
  Z & I & I & Z & I & I & I & Z & I & I & I & I & Z & I & I \\
  I & I & I & I & I & I & I & X & X & X & X & X & X & X & X \\
  I & I & I & X & X & X & X & I & I & I & I & X & X & X & X \\
  I & X & X & I & I & X & X & I & I & X & X & I & I & X & X \\
  X & I & X & I & X & I & X & I & X & I & X & I & X & I & X
\end{smallmatrix}~.
\end{align}
The logical \(\ket{\overline{0}}\) and \(\ket{\overline{1}}\) states are superpositions of computational-basis words of weight \(0 \bmod 8\) and \(7 \bmod 8\).

parents.detail: The \([[15,1,3]]\) code is a tetrahedral color code defined on a single tetrahedron.
The \([[15,1,3]]\) code can be viewed as an XS stabilizer code \cite[Exam. 6.4]{arxiv:2203.00103}.
The \([[15, 1, 3]]\) code is a triorthogonal code \cite{arxiv:2107.09684}.

cousins.detail: The \([[15,1,3]]\) code can be viewed as a (gauge-fixed) doubled color code obtained from the Steane code via the doubling transformation \cite{arxiv:1509.03239}.

The \([[15,1,3]]\) code can be viewed as a (gauge-fixed) doubled color code obtained from the Steane code via the doubling transformation \cite{arxiv:1509.03239}. A fault-tolerant universal gate set can be done via \hyperref[topic:code-switching]{code switching} between the Steane code and the \([[15,1,3]]\) code \cite{arxiv:1304.3709,arxiv:1403.2734,arxiv:1703.03860,arxiv:2210.14074,arxiv:2306.17686}. An \([[105,1,3]]\) alternative concatenation of the \([[15,1,3]]\) and Steane codes allows for a universal gate set consisting of gates that are close to transversal \cite{arxiv:1309.3310,arxiv:1710.07256}.

The \([[105,1]]\) concatenation of the \([[15,1,3]]\) and Steane codes allows for a universal gate set consisting of gates that are transversal w.r.t. to two different partitions \cite{arxiv:1309.3310,arxiv:1710.07256}.
The concatenated \([[15,1,3]]\) code has a \hyperref[topic:measurement-threshold]{measurement threshold} less than one \cite{arxiv:2402.00145}.
The \([[15,1,3]]\) code serves as the local tensor in a holographic quantum-Lego code on a \(\{15,4\}\) tiling that supports a transversal non-Clifford operator \cite{arxiv:2109.08158}.
The \([[15,1,3]]\) code serves as the local tensor in a holographic quantum-Lego code on a \(\{15,4\}\) tiling that supports a transversal non-Clifford operator \cite{arxiv:2109.08158}.
MBQC with the \([[15,1,3]]\) code has been demonstrated in neutral atom arrays by the Lukin group \cite{arxiv:2506.20661}.

realizations: Trapped ions: \hyperref[topic:code-switching]{code switching} between the Steane code and the \([[15,1,3]]\) code as well as magic-state preparation and logical Bell measurements on the Steane code realized on the 28-qubit H2-1 device by Quantinuum \cite{arxiv:2506.14169}.
MBQC with the \([[15,1,3]]\) code has been demonstrated in neutral atom arrays by the Lukin group \cite{arxiv:2506.20661}.

name: \([[15, 7, 3]]\) quantum Hamming code
description:
Self-dual quantum Hamming code that admits permutation-based CZ logical gates.
The code is constructed using the CSS construction from the \([15,11,3]\) Hamming code and its \([15,4,8]\) dual simplex code.

In the qubit order given by the nonzero binary four-tuples \(0001,0010,\ldots,1111\), one stabilizer tableau for the code is \cite[ID 6705229219cca60cf657a8fd]{preset:QECDB}
\begin{align}
\begin{smallmatrix}
  I & I & I & I & I & I & I & Z & Z & Z & Z & Z & Z & Z & Z \\
  I & I & I & Z & Z & Z & Z & I & I & I & I & Z & Z & Z & Z \\
  I & Z & Z & I & I & Z & Z & I & I & Z & Z & I & I & Z & Z \\
  Z & I & Z & I & Z & I & Z & I & Z & I & Z & I & Z & I & Z \\
  I & I & I & I & I & I & I & X & X & X & X & X & X & X & X \\
  I & I & I & X & X & X & X & I & I & I & I & X & X & X & X \\
  I & X & X & I & I & X & X & I & I & X & X & I & I & X & X \\
  X & I & X & I & X & I & X & I & X & I & X & I & X & I & X
\end{smallmatrix}~.
\end{align}

parents.detail: The \([[15,7,3]]\) is Hermitian \cite[ID 6705229219cca60cf657a8fd]{preset:QECDB}.

cousins.detail: \([[15, 7, 3]]\) quantum Hamming code is perfect as a CSS code, i.e., the number of its \(Z\)-type syndromes matches the number of \(X\)-type Pauli errors up to weight one \cite{arxiv:1705.05365}.
\hyperref[topic:gauging-out]{Gauging out} six of the seven logical qubits of the \([[15,7,3]]\) code yields the \([[15,1,3]]\) code \cite{arxiv:1410.0069}.

introduced: \cite{arxiv:quant-ph/9512032,arxiv:quant-ph/9605021,manual:{J. Harrington and B. W. Reichardt, “Addressable multi-qubit logic via permutations”, Talk at Southwest Quantum Information and Technology (SQuInT) (2011)}}

name: \([[12,1,3]]\) CE CSS code
description:
Twelve-qubit constant-excitation (CE) CSS code that encodes one logical qubit with distance three.
It is the smallest CE CSS code that corrects a single-qubit error \cite{arxiv:2507.10395}.
Codewords lie in a fixed Hamming-weight subspace, making the code immune to coherent noise in the form of transversal \(Z\)-rotations.

One stabilizer tableau for the code is, up to Pauli frame \cite{arxiv:2507.10395},

  I & I & I & I & I & I & I & I & X & X & X & X \\
  Z & I & Z & I & Z & I & Z & I & Z & I & Z & I \\
  Z & Z & I & I & I & I & I & I & I & I & I & I \\
  I & I & Z & Z & I & I & I & I & I & I & I & I \\
  I & I & I & I & Z & Z & I & I & I & I & I & I \\
  I & I & I & I & I & I & Z & Z & I & I & I & I \\
  I & I & I & I & I & I & I & I & Z & Z & I & I \\
  I & I & I & I & I & I & I & I & I & I & Z & Z
\end{array}~.
\end{align}

name: \([[12,2,4]]\) carbon code
description:
Twelve-qubit CSS code based on Knill's \(C_4/C_6\) scheme \cite{arxiv:2404.02280}.
Using the concatenation convention of the Zoo, the carbon code can be viewed as a block concatenation with inner code \([[4,2,2]]\) and outer code \(C_6\): three inner \([[4,2,2]]\) blocks encode six intermediate qubits, which are then encoded into two logical qubits by the outer \([[6,2,2]]\) code.

A stabilizer tableau for the code is given by \cite[Table IV]{arxiv:2404.02280}
\begin{align}
\begin{array}{cccccccccccc}
  X & X & X & X & I & I & I & I & I & I & I & I \\
  Z & Z & Z & Z & I & I & I & I & I & I & I & I \\
  I & I & I & I & X & X & X & X & I & I & I & I \\
  I & I & I & I & Z & Z & Z & Z & I & I & I & I \\
  I & I & I & I & I & I & I & I & X & X & X & X \\
  I & I & I & I & I & I & I & I & Z & Z & Z & Z \\
  X & X & I & I & I & X & I & X & X & I & I & X \\
  X & I & I & X & X & X & I & I & I & X & I & X \\
  Z & I & Z & I & I & I & Z & Z & Z & I & I & Z \\
  Z & I & I & Z & Z & I & Z & I & I & I & Z & Z
\end{array}~.
\end{align}

parents.detail: The carbon code is the B\&C phantom code obtained from the \([[3,1,2]]_4\) Galois-qudit code \cite{arxiv:2601.20927}.

cousins.detail: The carbon code is a concatenation of the \([[4,2,2]]\) code and the \(C_6\) code.
The carbon code is a concatenation of the \([[4,2,2]]\) code and the \(C_6\) code.

realizations: Trapped-ion devices: Three rounds of error correction and post-selected fault-tolerant logical Bell-state preparation with logical error rates at least 5 times lower than physical rate on a quantum charge-coupled device (QCCD) \cite{arxiv:2305.03828} by Microsoft and Quantinuum \cite{arxiv:2404.02280}.

name: \([[13,1,5]]\) quantum QR code
description:
Thirteen-qubit cyclic Hermitian qubit code derived from a quaternary quadratic-residue code using the Hermitian construction \cite[pg. 11]{arxiv:quant-ph/9704019}\cite{arxiv:2211.00891}.
The code admits a stabilizer tableau whose rows are cyclic permutations of the Pauli string \(XXZZIZIIIZIZZ\) \cite[ID 67067199e766ad364e845262]{preset:QECDB}.

name: \([[13,1,5]]\) twisted toric code
description:
Thirteen-qubit twisted toric code whose stabilizer tableau consists of cyclic permutations of the \(XZZX\)-type Pauli string \(XIZZIXIIIIIII\).
The code can be thought of as a small twisted XZZX code \cite[Exam. 11 and Fig. 3]{arxiv:1108.5490}.

parents.detail: The \([[13,1,5]]\) twisted toric code is a small twisted XZZX toric code \cite[Exam. 11 and Fig. 3]{arxiv:1108.5490}.
The \([[13,1,5]]\) twisted toric code is Hermitian \cite[ID 67067199e766ad364e845262]{preset:QECDB}.

name: \([[5,1,3]]\) Five-qubit perfect code

parents.detail: Twisted XZZX codes are 2D lattice extensions of the five-qubit perfect code. The five-qubit code is a small twisted XZZX toric code \cite[Exam. 11 and Fig. 3]{arxiv:1108.5490}\cite[Exam. 3]{arxiv:1212.6703}\cite[Fig. 1]{arxiv:2101.09349}. Its genus-one double cover is a \([[10,2,3]]\) toric code \cite[Exam. 3]{arxiv:1212.6703}\cite{arxiv:2406.09951}. The base code's transversal \(SH\) gate lifts to a logical \(CX \cdot SWAP\) gate on that double cover \cite{arxiv:2406.09951}.

The \((5,1,2)\)-convolutional code is a 1D lattice extension of the five-qubit perfect code, with the former's lattice-translation symmetry being the extension of the latter's cyclic permutation symmetry. The \((5,1,2)\)-convolutional code reduces to the five-qubit code for a five-qubit chain and periodic boundary conditions. See Ref. \cite{arxiv:2211.03094} for the first few codes in a different extension of the five-qubit perfect code.

The five-qubit code is the smallest (i.e., radius-one) single-qubit HaPPY code. The five-qubit encoding isometry tiles various holographic codes because its corresponding encoding isometry tensor is a \hyperref[topic:ame]{perfect tensor} \cite{arxiv:1503.06237}.
The five-qubit code is the smallest perfect code and is a member of the perfect qubit code family \([[(4^r-1)/3, (4^r-1)/3 - 2r, 3]]\) for \(r = 2\).

The five-qubit code is Hermitian \cite[ID 21]{preset:qiskit}, and is derived from the \([5,3,3]_4\) shortened hexacode via the \hyperref[code:stabilizer_over_gf4]{qubit Hermitian construction} \cite{arxiv:quant-ph/0310137}\cite[Exam. A]{arxiv:quant-ph/0511016}.
The only nontrivial qubit MDS codes have parameters \([[5,1,3]]\), \([[6,0,4]]\), and \([[2m,2m-2,2]]\) \cite[Sec. 27.4]{preset:HKSquantum}.
The \([[5,1,3]]\) code is the smallest qubit Frobenius code \cite[Table I]{arxiv:1011.5814}.
The \([[5,1,3]]_{\mathbb{Z}_q}\) modular-qudit code for \(q=2\) reduces to the five-qubit perfect code.
The \([[5,1,3]]_q\) Galois-qudit code for \(q=2\) reduces to the five-qubit perfect code.

cousins.detail: The five-qubit code is a group-representation code with \(G\) being the \(2T\) subgroup of \(SU(2)\) \cite{arxiv:2306.11621}.
The five-qubit code Hamiltonian is local when expressed in terms of mutually commuting Majorana operators \cite{manual:{A. Kubica, private communication, 2019}}.
Every \(((5,2,3))\) qubit code is single-qubit-Clifford-equivalent equivalent to the five-qubit code \cite[Corr. 10]{arxiv:quant-ph/9704043}.

The recursively concatenated five-qubit code has a \hyperref[topic:measurement-threshold]{measurement threshold} of one \cite{arxiv:2402.00145}. Code performance against general Pauli channels has been worked out \cite{arxiv:quant-ph/0111003,arxiv:quant-ph/0206061}.

The five-qubit perfect code is equivalent via a single-qubit \hyperref[topic:clifford]{Clifford circuit} to a cluster-state code defined from a five-cycle (a.k.a. pentagon) graph and a classical repetition code \cite{arxiv:0708.1021,preset:Cross09,preset:GriffithsGS,arxiv:1511.05647}\cite[Exam. 2]{arxiv:1108.5490}.
Inspired by the honeycomb Floquet code, various weight-two measurement schemes have been designed for the five-qubit code \cite{arxiv:2409.13681}.
The five-qubit perfect code approximately corrects a single \hyperref[topic:ad]{AD} error \cite{arxiv:quant-ph/9704002}.

A fault-tolerant logical \(T\) gate can be obtained by encoding the five-qubit code's five physical qubits into the five logical qubits of a \([[31,5,3]]\) outer quantum divisible CSS code preserved by transversal \(T^\dagger\); this layered construction can be viewed as a factorization of a \([[31,1,3]]\) triorthogonal code and does not require magic-state distillation \cite{arxiv:2204.13176}.

A fault-tolerant logical \(T\) gate can be obtained by encoding the five-qubit code's five physical qubits into the five logical qubits of a \([[31,5,3]]\) outer quantum divisible CSS code preserved by transversal \(T^\dagger\); this layered construction can be viewed as a factorization of a \([[31,1,3]]\) triorthogonal code and does not require magic-state distillation \cite{arxiv:2204.13176}.

realizations: NMR: Implementation of perfect error correcting code on 5 spin subsystem of labeled crotonic acid for quantum network benchmarking \cite{arxiv:quant-ph/0101034}. Single-qubit logical gates \cite{arxiv:1208.4797}. Magic-state distillation using 7-qubit device \cite{arxiv:1103.2178}.
Superconducting qubits \cite{arxiv:1907.04507}.

Trapped-ion qubits: non-transversal CNOT gate between two logical qubits, including rounds of correction and fault-tolerant primitives such as flag qubits and pieceable fault tolerance, on a 12-qubit device by Quantinuum \cite{arxiv:2208.01863}. Real-time magic-state distillation \cite{arxiv:2310.12106}.

Nitrogen-vacancy centers in diamond: fault-tolerant single-qubit Clifford operations using two ancillas \cite{arxiv:2108.01646}. The fault-tolerant circuit yields better fidelity than the non-fault-tolerant circuit.

name: \(((5,6,2))\) qubit code
description:
Five-qubit cyclic CWS code detecting a single-qubit error.
This code has a logical subspace whose dimension is larger than that of the \([[5,2,2]]\) code, the best five-qubit stabilizer code with the same distance \cite{arxiv:0709.1780}.

Its codeword stabilizer consists of all cyclic shifts of \(ZXZII\).
A standard-form CWS presentation uses the five-cycle graph together with the classical codewords \(00000\), \(11010\), \(01101\), \(10110\), \(01011\), and \(10101\) \cite{arxiv:0708.1021,arxiv:0709.1780}.
Its automorphism group is of size 3840 and given in Ref. \cite{arxiv:quant-ph/9703002} (see also \cite[Corr. 18]{arxiv:quant-ph/9704043}).

parents.detail: The \(((5,6,2))\) code is the smallest nontrivial Rains code \cite{arxiv:quant-ph/9704043} (see also \cite[Exam. 8]{arxiv:cs/0610159}\cite{arxiv:0801.0831}).
The \(((5,6,2))\) code is the \(((n,1+n(q-1),2))_q\) union stabilizer code for \(n=5\) and \(q=2\) \cite{arxiv:quant-ph/0210097}.

cousins.detail: Tracing out any one qubit of the \(((5,6,2))\) code projector yields a \(((4,4,2))\) code; for this code, all five such partial traces are additive and therefore locally equivalent to the \([[4,2,2]]\) code \cite[Thm. 8 and Corr. 18]{arxiv:quant-ph/9704043}.

name: \([[5,1,2]]\) rotated surface code

parents.detail: The \([[5,1,2]]\) code is a specific instance of the \([[2^r+r-1,1,2]]\) morphed simplex codes with \(r=2\) \cite[Fig. 1]{arxiv:2112.01446}.

The \([[5,1,2]]\) rotated surface code is the smallest SCF holographic code \cite{arxiv:2008.10206}. The encoding of more general SCF holographic codes is a holographic tensor network consisting of the encoding isometry for the \([[5,1,2]]\) rotated surface code, which is a \hyperref[code:block_perfect]{planar-perfect tensor}.

The \([[5,1,2]]\) rotated surface code is the smallest SCF holographic code \cite{arxiv:2008.10206}. The encoding of more general SCF holographic codes is a holographic tensor network consisting of the encoding isometry for the \([[5,1,2]]\) rotated surface code, which is a \hyperref[code:block_perfect]{planar-perfect tensor}.

cousins.detail: The \([[5,1,2]]\) rotated surface code is a genon code on a sphere, with the missing external \(Y\)-type stabilizer forming the back of the sphere. More generally, any surface code with a single boundary component can be interpreted this way \cite{arxiv:2406.09951}.
The \([[5,1,2]]\) morphed Steane code is obtained by morphing the Steane code on a region whose child code is a \([[4,2,2]]\) code \cite[Fig. 1]{arxiv:2112.01446}.
The \([[5,1,2]]\) morphed Steane code is obtained by morphing the Steane code on a region whose child code is a \([[4,2,2]]\) code \cite[Fig. 1]{arxiv:2112.01446}.

description:
CSS phantom code obtained by concatenating the \([[7,3,(d_X=3,d_Z=2)]]\) punctured hypercube code with the two-qubit phase-flip repetition code.
The code is equivalent to the \([[14,3,3]]\) constant-excitation (CE) CSS code obtained by applying dual-rail concatenation to the \([[7,3,2]]\) punctured hypercube code, up to single-qubit Clifford gates, a physical-qubit permutation, and a Pauli frame \cite{arxiv:2507.10395}.

  I & I & X & X & I & I & I & I & I & I & I & I & I & I \\
  I & I & I & I & X & X & I & I & I & I & I & I & I & I \\
  I & I & I & I & I & I & X & X & I & I & I & I & I & I \\
  I & I & I & I & I & I & I & I & X & X & I & I & I & I \\
  I & I & I & I & I & I & I & I & I & I & X & X & I & I \\
  I & I & I & I & I & I & I & I & I & I & I & I & X & X \\
  X & I & X & I & X & I & X & I & X & I & X & I & X & I
\end{smallmatrix}~.
\end{align}

parents.detail: This \([[14,3,3]]\) code is a CSS phantom code obtained from the punctured hypercube code and the two-qubit phase-flip repetition code \cite{arxiv:2601.20927}.
This code is single-qubit Clifford equivalent to the \([[14,3,3]]\) CE CSS code obtained by dual-rail concatenation of the \([[7,3,2]]\) punctured hypercube code \cite{arxiv:2507.10395}.
This code is a concatenation of the \([[7,3,2]]\) punctured hypercube code with the two-qubit phase-flip repetition code \cite{arxiv:2601.20927}.

cousins.detail: Concatenating the \([[7,3,(d_X=3,d_Z=2)]]\) punctured hypercube code with the two-qubit phase-flip repetition code yields this \([[14,3,(d_X=3,d_Z=4)]]\) CSS phantom code \cite{arxiv:2601.20927}. Dual-rail concatenation of the same punctured hypercube code yields a single-qubit Clifford-equivalent CE CSS frame \cite{arxiv:2507.10395}.

Dual-rail concatenation of the \([[7,1,3]]\) Steane code yields a \([[14,1,3]]\) CE CSS code, from which the locally Clifford-equivalent \([[14,3,3]]\) CE CSS frame is obtained by removing two independent \(Z\)-type stabilizer generators \cite{arxiv:2507.10395}.
The inner code in the construction is the two-qubit phase-flip repetition code \cite{arxiv:2601.20927}.

name: \([[14,3,3]]\) Rhombic dodecahedron surface code
description:
A \([[14,3,3]]\) twist-defect surface code whose qubits lie on the vertices of a rhombic dodecahedron.
Its non-CSS nature is due to twist defects \cite{arxiv:1004.1838} stemming from the geometry of the polytope.
A local-Clifford-equivalent clean realization has only \(X\)- and \(Z\)-type operators on its four-valent vertices, and its symplectic double is a \([[28,6,3]]\) genus-three code \cite{arxiv:2406.09951}.

A stabilizer tableau for the code is \cite[Eq. (1)]{arxiv:2010.06628}
\begin{align}
\begin{smallmatrix}
  X & X & X & I & I & X & I & I & I & I & I & I & I & I \\
  I & I & X & X & I & I & X & X & I & I & I & I & I & I \\
  I & I & I & I & I & I & I & X & X & I & I & X & X & I \\
  X & I & I & I & I & I & I & I & I & X & I & I & X & X \\
  I & Y & Y & Y & Y & I & I & I & I & I & I & I & I & I \\
  I & I & Y & I & I & Y & Y & I & I & I & Y & I & I & I \\
  I & I & I & I & Y & I & I & I & Y & Y & I & I & Y & I \\
  I & I & I & I & I & I & I & I & I & I & Y & Y & Y & Y \\
  Z & Z & I & I & Z & I & I & I & I & Z & I & I & I & I \\
  I & I & I & Z & Z & I & I & Z & Z & I & I & I & I & I \\
  I & I & I & I & I & I & Z & Z & I & I & Z & Z & I & I
\end{smallmatrix}~.
\end{align}

parents.detail: The rhombic dodecahedron surface code is a twist-defect surface code whose degree-three vertices can be interpreted as disclination twists \cite{arxiv:2010.06628}.

cousins.detail: The qubits of the \([[14,3,3]]\) rhombic dodecahedron surface code lie on the vertices of the small rhombic dodecahedron.

name: Subsystem spacetime circuit code
description:
Subsystem stabilizer code obtained from a spacetime circuit code by \hyperref[topic:gauging-out]{gauging out} logical operators that correspond to circuit faults with trivial effect \cite[Sec. 5.4]{arxiv:2304.05943}.
In the original circuit-to-code construction, each circuit element is replaced by low-weight gauge generators enforcing its input-output relations, yielding subsystem codes from restricted Clifford postselection circuits \cite{arxiv:1411.3334}.

An \([[n,k,d]]\) stabilizer code can be mapped into a sparse subsystem code with the same \(k\) and \(d\) as follows.
One can take the fault-tolerant syndrome extraction circuit associated with the stabilizer code, construct its spacetime circuit code, and then \hyperref[topic:gauging-out]{gauge out} qubits corresponding to trivial faults.
The subsystem code can be made geometrically local at the cost of more ancilla qubits \cite{arxiv:1411.3334}.

cousins.detail: Spacetime circuit codes can yield subsystem spacetime circuit codes by \hyperref[topic:gauging-out]{gauging out} a subgroup of the logical \hyperref[topic:pauli]{Pauli group} which causes trivial faults in the corresponding \hyperref[topic:clifford]{Clifford circuit}. This construction is used to show the existence of geometrically local subsystem codes that nearly saturate the \hyperref[topic:subsystem-bt-bound]{subsystem BT bound} \cite{arxiv:1411.3334}.

name: Subsystem qubit code
description: Subsystem QECC encoding into a \(2^n\)-dimensional (i.e., \(n\)-qubit) Hilbert space.

parents.detail: An OA qubit code which has gauge structure (e.g., gauge qubits) but no block structure is a subsystem qubit code.
Subsystem modular-qudit codes reduce to subsystem qubit codes for qudit dimension \(q=2\).
Subsystem Galois-qudit quantum codes for \(q=2\) correspond to subsystem qubit codes.

cousins.detail: Subsystem qubit codes reduce to (subspace) qubit codes when there is no gauge subsystem.

name: Subsystem qubit CSS code
description:
Subsystem qubit stabilizer code which admits a set of gauge-group generators which consist of either all-\(Z\) or all-\(X\) Pauli strings.
This ensures that the code's stabilizer group is also CSS.

The gauge-group generators can be expressed as a matrix using the symplectic representation. This matrix is of the form
\begin{align}
G=\begin{pmatrix}0 & G_{Z}\\
G_{X} & 0
\end{pmatrix}~.
\label{eq:parity}
\end{align}
The two matrix blocks, \(G_{Z}\) and \(G_X\), correspond to the parity-check matrices of two \hyperref[code:binary_linear]{binary linear codes}, an \([n,k_X,d_X]\) code \(C_X\) and an \([n,k_Z,d_Z]\) code \(C_Z\), respectively.
Code parameters can be expressed in terms of only data associated with these two classical codes \cite{arxiv:quant-ph/0610153,arxiv:quant-ph/0604161}.
Explicit basis-state constructions are given in Ref. \cite{arxiv:2311.18003}.

parents.detail: Subsystem qubit CSS codes are subsystem qubit stabilizer codes whose gauge groups admit a generating set of pure-\(X\) and pure-\(Z\) Pauli strings. Any \([[n,k,r,d]]\) subsystem qubit stabilizer code can be mapped onto a \([[2n,2k,2r,\geq d]]\) subsystem CSS code via \hyperref[topic:symplectic-doubling]{symplectic doubling}, which preserves geometric locality of a code up to a constant factor. Every subsystem qubit stabilizer code can be constructed from two nested subsystem CSS codes satisfying certain constraints \cite{arxiv:2311.18003}.
Subsystem modular-qudit CSS codes reduce to subsystem qubit CSS codes for \(q=2\).
Subsystem Galois-qudit CSS codes reduce to subsystem qubit CSS codes for \(q=2\).

cousins.detail: Subsystem qubit CSS codes reduce to (subspace) CSS qubit codes when there is no gauge subsystem.

align_blocks:
G=\begin{pmatrix}0 & G_{Z}\\
G_{X} & 0
\end{pmatrix}~.
\label{eq:parity}

name: Subsystem holographic code
description: A holographic tensor-network code constructed out of alternating isometries of the five-qubit and \([[4,1,1,2]]\) Bacon-Shor codes.

parents.detail: The holographic hybrid code is constructed out of alternating isometries of the five-qubit and \([[4,1,1,2]]\) Bacon-Shor codes.
The holographic hybrid code is constructed out of alternating isometries of the five-qubit and \([[4,1,1,2]]\) Bacon-Shor codes.

cousins.detail: The holographic hybrid code is constructed out of alternating isometries of the five-qubit and \([[4,1,1,2]]\) Bacon-Shor codes.
The holographic hybrid code is constructed out of alternating isometries of the five-qubit and \([[4,1,1,2]]\) Bacon-Shor codes.

name: Subsystem qubit stabilizer code
description:
A stabilizer code with some of its logical qubits denoted as \textit{gauge} qubits and not used for storage of logical information.
Note that this doesn't lead to new codes but does lead to new error correction and fault tolerance procedures.
Subsystem codes are denoted by \([[n,k,g,d]]\), similar to stabilizer codes, but with an extra parameter \(g\) denoting the number of gauge qubits.

Subsystem qubit stabilizer codes are defined by a gauge group \(\mathsf{G}\), together with a stabilizer subgroup \(\mathsf{S}\) of the center of \(\mathsf{G}\).
The table below summarizes the relevant groups and their sizes for a subsystem qubit stabilizer code.
  \begin{table}
    \begin{cells}
    \celldata<c H, c H, c H>{purpose & symbol & size}
    \celldata<c, c, c>{
    gauge group & \(\mathsf{G}\) & \(4\cdot 2^{n-k+g}\)
        \\
    stabilizer group & \(\mathsf{S}\) & \(2^{n-k-g}\)
        \\
    code-preserving Paulis & \(\mathsf{N}(\mathsf{S})\) & \(4\cdot 2^{n+k+g}\)
        \\
    logical Paulis & \(\mathsf{N}(\mathsf{S})/\mathsf{G}\) & \(4^{k}\)
        \\
    gauge Paulis & \(\frac{\mathsf{N}(\mathsf{S})}{\mathsf{N}(\mathsf{S})/\mathsf{G}\times\left\langle i,\mathsf{S}\right\rangle }=\mathsf{G}/\left\langle i,\mathsf{S}\right\rangle\) & \(4^{g}\)
        \\
    gauge-preserving Paulis & \(\mathsf{N}(\mathsf{G})\) & \(4\cdot 2^{n+k-g}\)
        \\
    bare logicals & \(\mathsf{N}(\mathsf{G})/\left\langle i,\mathsf{S}\right\rangle\) & \(4^{k}\)
    }
    \end{cells}
    \caption{Groups relevant to subsystem qubit stabilizer codes. The centralizer \(\mathsf{N}(\mathsf{S})\) is the group formed by all elements of the \(n\)-qubit Pauli group that commute with all elements in \(\mathsf{S}\). 
    The notation \(\mathsf{N}\) is used because the centralizer of a stabilizer group \(\mathsf{S}\) is the same as the group's normalizer, the group formed by all elements of the \(n\)-qubit Pauli group that conjugate \(\mathsf{S}\) to itself.
    This equivalence is no longer the case for non-stabilizer Pauli groups, e.g., the normalizer of the Pauli group is the group itself, while its centralizer is just \(\langle i \rangle\).
    The gauge group and centralizer are defined so as to include \(i\) and its powers as elements, while the stabilizer group is not.}
    \label{table:subsystem-groups}
  \end{table}

To create these codes proceed as follows.
Choose \(2n\) operators \(\{ \tilde{X}_j,\tilde{Z}_j\}_{j=1}^n\) from \(\mathsf{P}_n\), the \hyperref[topic:pauli]{Pauli group} on \(n\) qubits, such that they obey the same commutation relations as the regular \(n\)-qubit Pauli generators \( \{X_j,Z_j\}_{j=1}^n \) (the subscript on these latter operators indicates the single qubit the Pauli matrix acts on).
The tilde operators might act on more than one physical (or \textit{bare}) qubit but they behave as if they acted only on a single qubit.
WLOG we can choose a stabilizer group as \( \mathsf{S} = \langle \tilde{Z}_1,\dots, \tilde{Z}_s \rangle \). It follows that the normalizer of \(\mathsf{S} \) is \( \mathsf{N}(\mathsf{S}) = \langle i, \tilde{Z}_1,\dots, \tilde{Z}_n, \tilde{X}_{s+1},\dots, \tilde{X}_n \rangle \).
We now choose a gauge group as \( \mathsf{G} = \langle i, \tilde{Z}_1,\dots, \tilde{Z}_s, \tilde{X}_{s+1}, \tilde{Z}_{s+1}, \dots, \tilde{X}_{s+g}, \tilde{Z}_{s+g} \rangle \) with \( s + g \leq n \).
The logical group is chosen as \( \mathsf{L} = \mathsf{N}(\mathsf{S})/\mathsf{G} \simeq \langle \tilde{X}_{s+g+1},\tilde{Z}_{s+g+1}, \dots, \tilde{X}_n,\tilde{Z}_n \rangle \).
